---
# ── Listing card fields ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
title: "The Effect of Corporate Taxes on Investment and Entrepreneurship"
author: "Djankov, Ganser, McLiesh, Ramalho & Shleifer"
date: "2010"
date-format: "YYYY"
description: "Cross-country OLS: do higher corporate tax rates reduce investment and entrepreneurship?"
categories:
  - OLS
  - Public Finance
  - Taxation
  - "2010"
  - PARTIAL
image: forest_plot.png

# ── Paper metadata ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
paper-journal: "American Economic Journal: Macroeconomics"
paper-doi: "10.1257/mac.2.3.31"
paper-url: ""

# ── Replication results ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
replication-status: "PARTIAL"
replication-delta-pct: 177.9

# ── DML results ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
dml-estimate: -0.204
dml-ci-lo: -0.388
dml-ci-hi: -0.020
dml-preferred-learner: "Best (Random Forest)"
dml-shift: "downward"

# ── Causal Forest results ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cf-estimate: null
cf-ci-lo: null
cf-ci-hi: null
cf-method: ""
cf-pct-significant: null

# ── Review process ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
rounds-completed: 1
final-verdict: "Minor manual revision needed"
---

## Paper summary

**Citation:** Djankov, S., Ganser, T., McLiesh, C., Ramalho, R. & Shleifer, A. (2010). "The Effect of Corporate Taxes on Investment and Entrepreneurship." *American Economic Journal: Macroeconomics*, 2(3), 31–64. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1257/mac.2.3.31)

**Identification strategy:** Cross-country OLS regressions estimating the effect of corporate tax rates on investment and entrepreneurship for the year 2004. The paper uses three measures of corporate taxes (statutory rate, first-year effective rate, five-year effective rate) and four outcome variables (investment/GDP, FDI/GDP, business density, entry rate), with 12 control variables spanning tax burden, business regulation, governance, and inflation.

**Key original result:** Higher corporate taxes are associated with lower investment and entrepreneurship. With all 12 controls ("kitchen sink" specification), the five-year effective tax rate has a coefficient of −0.189 (SE 0.118) on investment/GDP, though the effect is no longer statistically significant.

**Reference for this RECAST:** This paper was also revisited by [Baiardi & Naghi (2024, *Econometrics Journal*)](https://doi.org/10.1093/ectj/utae004), who applied DML with 7 ML methods to the same data and found larger, often significant negative effects.

---

## Replication results

The replication is **partially successful**. All 12 OLS coefficients (4 outcomes × 3 tax measures) have the correct **negative sign**. However, coefficient magnitudes differ from the Baiardi & Naghi (2024) reference due to a sample-size gap: our data has N = 42–53 after listwise deletion vs. N = 50–61 in the reference, driven by missing values in institutional control variables (employment rigidity, legal formalism, average informality).

| Specification (effective_5yr) | Published | Replicated | Δ (%) | Status |
|-------------------------------|-----------|------------|-------|--------|
| Panel A: Investment/GDP       | −0.189    | −0.089     | 53.0% | FAIL   |
| Panel B: FDI/GDP              | −0.095    | −0.121     | 27.9% | FAIL   |
| Panel C: Business density     | −0.070    | −0.195     | 177.9%| FAIL   |
| Panel D: Entry rate           | −0.133    | −0.128     | 3.5%  | PASS   |

![Forest plot: OLS vs. DML estimates across methods](forest_plot.png)

---

## DML Extension

Double/Debiased Machine Learning was applied using the **PLR** (Partially Linear Regression) model with adaptive cross-fitting (K=2 folds for N<200), 4 ML methods (LassoCV, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, ElasticNet), and 3 repetitions. All 12 specifications (4 outcomes × 3 tax measures) were estimated.

### Key results (five-year effective tax rate)

| Panel | OLS | Lasso | Boosting | Forest | ElasticNet | Best |
|-------|-----|-------|----------|--------|------------|------|
| A: Investment | −0.089 | +0.010 | −0.132 | −0.118 | +0.027 | −0.132 |
| B: FDI | −0.121 | −0.120 | −0.139 | −0.163* | −0.148** | −0.163* |
| C: Biz density | −0.195 | −0.247*** | −0.104 | −0.165** | −0.177** | −0.247*** |
| D: Entry rate | −0.128 | −0.173** | −0.228*** | −0.204** | −0.078 | −0.228*** |

*Stars: \* p<0.10, \*\* p<0.05, \*\*\* p<0.01*

**Best learner:** Selected by lowest nuisance MSE (not p-value).

**Key finding:** DML recovers significant negative tax effects for **3 of 4 outcomes** (FDI, business density, entry rate), where OLS with all controls was insignificant. This confirms and strengthens the original paper's finding that corporate taxes reduce investment and entrepreneurship. For investment/GDP (Panel A), DML is uninformative — two learners flip sign due to deeply negative nuisance R² (the sample is too small for reliable ML estimation with 12 controls).

### GATE analysis

Group Average Treatment Effects were estimated by quartiles of `other_taxes`. No significant heterogeneity was detected — all GATE CIs overlap widely, consistent with the small sample size (N ≈ 50).

![GATE estimates by group](gate_plot.png)

---

## What did causal ML add here?

The DML extension adds genuine value for this paper, despite the challenging setting (N ≈ 50, 12 controls). The original "kitchen sink" OLS specification, where all controls are included simultaneously, produces insignificant results because the high dimensionality relative to the sample size inflates standard errors. DML's data-driven variable selection (via Lasso) and flexible nonlinear control (via Boosting and Random Forest) keep a smaller set of influential confounders, leading to **larger absolute coefficients and lower standard errors** — exactly the mechanism Baiardi & Naghi (2024) identify.

The main limitation is Panel A (investment/GDP), where the nuisance models perform worse than predicting the mean (negative R²). This is a known challenge when N/p is close to 1. The paper honestly flags this, recommending that only tree-based DML estimates be considered for Panel A.

The GATE analysis found no heterogeneity, which is unsurprising given the sample size. A causal forest extension (not run in this RECAST) could provide individual-level CATEs if applied to the larger pooled dataset.

---

## Referee reports

**Referee consensus:** All three referees assigned "Major concerns" in round 1, with 7 major and 9 minor issues — all resolved through prose edits in a single revision round. No notebook re-runs required. The final verdict is "Minor manual revision needed" for 4 remaining items (1 major, 3 minor).

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Identification

{{< include reports/ref1.md >}}

## DML Methods

{{< include reports/ref2.md >}}

## Robustness

{{< include reports/ref3.md >}}

## Synthesis

{{< include reports/synthesis.md >}}

## Final Report

{{< include reports/final_report.md >}}

:::
